patch.content:
## The Analytical Brain

The intelligence module is where SproutLab stops being a logger and becomes an analyst. Individual domain modules — diet, sleep, medical — each track raw data in their own context. The intelligence module sits above all of them, finding patterns that no single domain could reveal.

The core insight driving the module’s design: baby care data is most valuable when domains talk to each other. A parent can see that Ziva slept badly last night. They can see that she ate less than usual yesterday. But without the intelligence module, they can’t easily see that she consistently sleeps worse on days when her protein intake is below a threshold — a correlation that only emerges when feeding and sleep data are analyzed together.

## Intelligence-Aware Scoring

Every day receives a composite score using an 80/20 base-modifier architecture. The 80% base comes from five domain scores: feeding consistency, sleep quality, digestion health, activity level, and medical status. Each domain score is normalized to 0–100 based on domain-specific criteria — feeding consistency rewards regular intervals and adequate volume, sleep quality measures total duration and wake frequency against age-appropriate baselines.

The 20% modifier adjusts for factors that span domains: trend direction (is this week better or worse than last week?), consistency (are the patterns stable or erratic?), and cross-domain correlations (does a change in one domain explain a change in another?). The modifier can boost a mediocre day that’s part of an improving trend, or flag a seemingly good day that breaks a concerning pattern.

A day where Ziva slept well, ate consistently, and had normal digestion scores higher than a day with one spectacular nap but erratic everything else. The system rewards stability over peaks because infant development thrives on predictable rhythms.

## The Intelligence Service Layer

The ISL is the computational engine behind the intelligence module. It provides temporal query parsing (understanding "last week" vs "the past 3 days"), domain data accessors (uniform interfaces to each module’s data), and day summary generation. The ISL powers both the intelligence cards on the home screen and the Smart Q&A system.

Key functions include generateRangeSummary() for multi-day analysis, getDomainScore() for individual domain evaluation, and detectCorrelations() for cross-domain pattern mining. The ISL was designed to be extended — adding a new analysis type means writing a new accessor function, not restructuring the pipeline.

## Evidence-Based Milestones

506 classification patterns across four developmental domains: motor (gross and fine), cognitive, language, and social-emotional. Each milestone has three states: upcoming (age-appropriate but not yet observed), emerging (partially demonstrated), and achieved (consistently demonstrated).

The auto-promotion engine watches activity logs and suggests milestone transitions. When Ziva started pulling to stand using furniture consistently, the motor milestone was promoted from emerging to achieved without manual intervention. The engine uses frequency thresholds — a behavior observed once might be a fluke, but observed five times across three days is a milestone.

The milestone system doesn’t diagnose or raise alarms. It tracks and visualizes. The interpretation belongs to parents and pediatricians. SproutLab provides the data; humans provide the judgment.
